title: 2025/11/13 - ENV
displayTitle: 2025-11-12 - Environment Committee - Minister's response to the emissions reduction monitoring report for 2025
topic: Minister's response to the emissions reduction monitoring report for 2025
description: Thursday, 13th November 2025 | Environment Committee Minister's response to the emissions reduction monitoring report for 2025 Hearing of evidence: Minister of Climate Change, Hon Simon Watts
descriptionSummary: Minister's response to the emissions reduction monitoring report for 2025. Minister of Climate Change, Hon Simon Watts

chunks.text: No, it was really good.
Ah, very good morning everyone. Good morning. See you.

Alright, the cast. Alright. Did it last one in. Thank you. Alright. Good morning. Um, minister, um, right, the Climate Change Commission publishes Emissions Reduction Monitoring Report. The Minister for Climate Change must table formal response in the House.

He did so back in October. Sponsors automatically referred to the Environment Committee, which has 60 working days to examine it and report to the house, uh, by 3rd of January, environment invite committee invited several public, um, sector organizations to make written comment on this response. The invitation leaders are listed, their submissions are under 2.2 and will be released after today's meeting environment.

Also invited the minister for a 30 minute hearing to talk about, and this is what we are doing now. So, over to you, Simon. Well, thank you very much. Happy remarks, and we'll go for questions. Excellent. Thank you very much, uh, and very good morning, uh, chair and committee members. Thanks for the opportunity to address, uh, the inquiry and I'm sure everyone is anxious to ask questions, so I'll keep my, uh, remarks, uh, very brief.

Uh, and then we'll get into some q and a. First and foremost, wanna introduce, uh, two of the team. We've got Sam Buckle here, who's the Deputy Secretary for the Ministry for the Environment, and also, uh, Kirsty Flanagan, who's the executive, uh, director. Both are heavily involved in terms of, uh, this report. Uh, so this is an inquiry into the government's response to the Commission's 2025 emissions, uh, reduction monitoring report.

Uh, I'm happy to take any specific questions you have about, uh, the government's response. I want to emphasize that for a large part, uh, we support the commission's findings. Uh, I'd note too that our response drew on the 2025 emissions projections, which weren't available to the commission, uh, when it were prepared. Uh, its report, the commission's report, uh, found that New Zealand is likely to meet its first emissions budget and can meet its second, uh, budget.

Our assessment informed by the 2025 projections confirms that we are on track, uh, to meet Target nine, uh, and our first and second emissions budgets. The government's most recent projections released, uh, last month have us, uh, coming in at 7.8 million ton under the first emissions budget and 4.5 million ton under the second emissions budget.

That's a, a larger buffer than what was projected in 2024, reflecting an improved progress towards our climate change targets. While the 2025 projections show a gap of 7.9 million ton to meet the third emissions budget, that gap is actually reduced by 1.3 million ton compared to where we were in 2024.

In the projections, I want to create a future where our economy and our communities thrive and where we can deliver value, uh, for money in the climate transition. The government can't deliver our climate goals alone. Uh, the private sector has a significant role to play, which is why we're working with some of New Zealand's largest industries to support them, to decarbonize a range of factors impacting our progress towards a target.

Some of those are not, uh, within our control, but some are obviously, and that's why it's important that we continue to review and refine our climate policy settings to ensure that they remain effective and practical. My final point, uh, Mr. Chair, is, is that staying, this is all about, uh, ensuring that we are staying on track. The second emissions reduction plan includes an adaptive management approach, which tracks progress.

It reviews the risks that we face, and also importantly, enables timely action for us to keep on track to meet the second emissions budget. And with that, uh, thank you, uh, Mr. Chair. I'm happy to take questions from the committee. Thank You. If I have just, uh, two people have hands up for questions, uh, Deborah and then Chloe. So I'll start with Deborah.

Thank you. Um, thank you minister. Um, minister looking, uh, page 11 of your response. You talk about using adaptive management. Can you explain what you mean by adaptive management and, Yes. So when we refer to adaptive management, it's taken to account, uh, how, uh, things are changing, uh, in the context of emissions reduction within the New Zealand, uh, economy.

I'll give you an example of that. Uh, this morning on RNZ, and this is real time, um, there was an interview with Eco Pond. And Eco Pond, uh, noted that they are in the process of rolling at a pilot of that on farm. Uh, however, they have now have the intention of, uh, as quoted going to a thousand farms in the next few years.

Uh, well, in our emissions reduction plan that was published last year, we had an expectations that there would be 250 dairy farms having that technology by the mid 2030s. And so that's just an example, again, outside of government control, but how industry and those factors are, and that's obviously has a quite a significant play in regards to methane reduction for, so that's an example of adapters management.

Things change. We reassess that. We make sure that our plans are reflective of the best and available information. So what sort of timeframes do you envisage your adaptive management working over? Um, well, in effect, we have a, an ongoing process, uh, in the context of taking on board such as events, uh, uh, today.

Uh, and we are continuing and as minister continuing assessing what options. We have formalized processes where we need to update our missions reduction plan, which is a standard governance process, and we do a consultation with the public on that. Uh, we also have governance mechanisms, uh, within, um, the ministerial groups. I have accountability for delivery of the targets, but minister of agriculture, minister of Transport have responsibility.

So there's a matrix, uh, of ministers that are involved in that. And we, uh, you know, catch up on a, uh, formal basis. And then we've got target nine, which is the prime ministerial targets, and that is a quarterly reporting cycle as well. So, So one of the things that concerns me is a, a lot of the work that need we need to do in the, um, particularly in the removal space, is it's quite, takes quite a long time.

So, you know, a forest takes about five years from planting until when it starts to actually sequester a significant amount of carbon and things like that. So, uh, worried that the timeframes we need to operate over don't really match up with this adaptive management. It sounds like, um, the, we need to be thinking not just sort of immediate adaptation to what's going on, but things that are happening out 5, 10, 15, 20 years.

So how does this adaptive management work with the fact that we operating over quite long time scales as well? Sure. It's definitely a case that the, The seeds we plant today have enduring benefit and, uh, over the long term, uh, in that context. But in terms of adaptive management, the, the commission sites, uh, examples in their report of what they would expect, uh, a government would be looking at in regards to adaptive management.

They refer particularly around agricultural technology innovation, uh, as an area acknowledging there is huge progress and pace. Uh, that is definitely an area where the government is looking at. They also cite, uh, areas in regards to, uh, energy, uh, and renewable energy and battery technology and things like that.

Again, you know, the electrify New Zealand and the RMA reforms that we're doing are making it a lot easier to build new generation of renewable energy. So, so that's what the, the, um, commission have cited is where the government, you know, as examples of what they would like to see the government doing in regard to adaptive management. I'm now providing, you know, examples of what the government is doing, which shows that there is, you know, quite a degree of alignment around that.

It is, to your point, a challenging area and any forward look is always comes with a degree of uncertainty because of forecast. But you know, it is fair to say that, uh, you know, as a government, we need to be doing every you as much as we can in the context of delivery here and now to lock in those benefits in the future. Okay. Not 30, not 53. The numbers are there, they're in the projections. We are making more progress than what we thought at this point last year. That's the numbers.
